goal_title_suggestions: Follow a Real Program and Grow

variants: Download either Strong or Hevy right now and create your free account.
Open the App Store, install Strong or Hevy, and sign up in the next 2 minutes.
Pick one workout logging app — Strong or Hevy — and get it installed on your phone today.
tasks.why: You need a log before you lift a single weight — no log means no progressive overload.

variants: Open the r/Fitness wiki or search 'GZCLP' or 'StrongLifts 5x5' and decide which free program you'll run — write the name in your notes app.
Choose your program right now: GZCLP, StrongLifts 5x5, or r/Fitness PPL — read the overview of each and commit to one by writing its name down.
Spend 4 minutes scanning GZCLP, StrongLifts 5x5, and r/Fitness PPL, then pick one and save the program link to your notes.
tasks.why: Program selection is the first decision — commit now so you stop browsing and start training.

variants: Step on the scale right now, log your weight in your notes, then take 3 photos — front, side, and back — and save them to a private album labeled 'Day 1'.
Weigh yourself, write the number down, and shoot 3 starting photos (front, back, left side) — store them in a folder you'll actually keep.
Get your Day 1 baseline locked in: bodyweight on the scale, 3 photos from different angles, both saved somewhere you can compare in 90 days.
tasks.why: Baseline data is the only way to see real progress — take it before the first workout.

variants: Calculate your protein target: take your bodyweight in pounds and multiply by 0.8 — write that number down as your daily gram goal.
Figure out your protein number right now: bodyweight (lbs) × 0.8 = daily grams — put it in your notes next to your Day 1 weight.
Get your protein target locked in today — bodyweight in pounds times 0.8, written somewhere you'll see it every morning.
tasks.why: Protein is the single most important nutrition variable for muscle — know your number before you eat another meal.

variants: Download MyFitnessPal, create your account, and log everything you've eaten today so far — even if it's just coffee.
Open MyFitnessPal, set up your profile, and enter at least one meal from today to get comfortable with the logging flow.
Install MyFitnessPal right now, create the account, and log your last full meal with actual gram amounts.
tasks.why: Hitting protein daily requires tracking — get the tool set up before tomorrow's first full tracking day.

variants: Read your chosen program's full overview — every workout, every lift listed — then write out Week 1 Day 1's exact exercises, sets, and reps in Strong or Hevy.
Open your program (GZCLP, StrongLifts, or PPL) and manually enter your first workout session into Strong or Hevy so it's ready to go when you walk into the gym.
Map out your first training session in your workout app right now: name each exercise, set the number of sets and reps, and add the starting weights you'll use.
tasks.why: Walking in with a plan loaded means zero wasted time — you lift instead of figuring out what to do.

variants: Set a repeating alarm for your workout days this week — label it with the session name (e.g., 'Push Day') so there's no deciding when you wake up.
Open your calendar and block your first 3 training sessions this week as non-negotiable appointments with the gym or home space noted.
Schedule your workouts for the next 7 days right now — specific days, specific times — and add them to your phone calendar.
tasks.why: Scheduled workouts happen; unscheduled ones get skipped when life hits.

variants: Estimate your TDEE using a free calculator like TDEE.cc — enter your stats and write down your maintenance calories and your +250 surplus target.
Go to TDEE.cc or a similar calculator, plug in your age, weight, height, and activity level, then note your TDEE and add 250 to get your daily calorie target.
Calculate your calorie surplus target now: use any free TDEE calculator, get your maintenance number, add 250, and save both numbers in your notes.
tasks.why: Eating 200-300 over maintenance fuels muscle growth without unnecessary fat gain — you need the number to hit it.

variants: Do your first real workout today using your logged program — execute every set, every rep, and log the actual weight used in Strong or Hevy before you leave.
Execute Session 1 of your chosen program right now — every exercise, every set — and log each lift's weight and reps in your tracking app as you go.
Get under the bar (or hit the floor) for your first programmed session — run it start to finish and record every set in Strong or Hevy before you're done.
tasks.why: The first session is the hardest one to start — completing it proves the habit is real.

variants: Log yesterday's full food intake in MyFitnessPal if you haven't already — check whether you hit your protein target.
Open MyFitnessPal and finish logging Day 3's meals — note if you hit your protein gram target or fell short.
Pull up MyFitnessPal right now and complete yesterday's food log — see how close you got to your protein number.
tasks.why: Reviewing yesterday closes the feedback loop — you can't improve what you don't check.

variants: Find one high-protein food you actually like that you're not eating regularly — add it to your next grocery list or order it today.
Identify one protein source you're underusing (chicken breast, Greek yogurt, cottage cheese, eggs) and add it to today's meal plan explicitly.
Pick one easy protein add-on — a shake, Greek yogurt, or extra eggs — and add it to your eating plan for today to close any protein gap.
tasks.why: Hitting 0.8g/lb daily requires deliberate food choices, not luck.

variants: Do your next programmed session today — hit every set, log every lift, and note if any exercise felt too light so you know to add weight next time.
Execute your second training session per your program's schedule — log it in Strong or Hevy and flag any sets where you had 3+ reps in reserve at the top.
Train today per your program — run each set, track each weight in your app, and mark any lift where the weight was clearly too easy for you.
tasks.why: Consistency in week 1 sets the baseline for everything that follows.

variants: Weigh yourself this morning before eating and log the number — track it in the same notes file as Day 1.
Step on the scale first thing, no food or water yet, and write the number next to your Day 1 baseline.
Get a morning weigh-in right now and add it to your tracking notes — you're building a weekly data set, not a single number.
tasks.why: Weekly weigh-ins reveal trends — daily fluctuations are noise, weekly averages are signal.

variants: Log all your meals in MyFitnessPal today and make sure protein hits your target before dinner — if you're short, add a shake or high-protein snack.
Track every meal in MyFitnessPal today in real time, not at the end of the day — hit your protein number before 8pm.
Open MyFitnessPal after each meal today and log it immediately — check your running protein total at lunch and again at dinner.
tasks.why: Same-day logging catches shortfalls while you can still fix them.

variants: Watch one 5-min YouTube video on the primary compound lift you struggled with most in session 1, pause it, and immediately practice the cue mentioned in your next set.
Find a form tutorial under 6 minutes for your weakest lift this week, watch it fully, then write down 2 cues to apply next session.
Pick the exercise from your first two sessions that felt most awkward, find a technique video under 5 minutes for it, and write 2 specific things to fix next time.
tasks.why: Form locked in early prevents injuries that kill programs before week 4.

variants: Plan your meals for tomorrow in MyFitnessPal tonight — pre-log breakfast, lunch, and dinner so hitting protein tomorrow requires no decisions.
Pre-log tomorrow's food in MyFitnessPal right now — full meals, estimated portions — so you wake up knowing exactly what to eat.
Open MyFitnessPal and build tomorrow's meal plan tonight — log everything in advance so your protein target is already guaranteed.
tasks.why: Pre-logging eliminates the decision fatigue that causes protein shortfalls.

variants: Execute your next training session today — follow the program exactly, use the weights you logged before, and record each set in Strong or Hevy.
Hit your scheduled workout today: run every set per the program, log every weight in your tracking app, and note your RPE after the hardest set.
Train today per your program — no substitutions, no skips — and update your workout log in Strong or Hevy immediately after each exercise.
tasks.why: Three sessions in the first week locks in the habit before motivation fades.

variants: Look up the progressive overload rule for your specific program — GZCLP, StrongLifts, or PPL — and write down exactly when and how much you add weight.
Find your program's progression rules right now (e.g., 'add 5 lbs to upper body lifts each session') and put them in your notes so you apply them automatically.
Read your program's weight progression instructions carefully and write a one-line rule for each main lift — you'll follow this rule every single session.
tasks.why: Knowing your progression rule means you add weight confidently instead of guessing.

variants: Review your workout log from the past 6 days — count how many sessions you completed vs. planned and write 'Hit X of Y sessions' in your notes.
Open Strong or Hevy and look at all sessions logged this week — note how many you completed and whether any lifts felt significantly easier than session 1.
Check your training log for the week: how many sessions done, which lifts went up, which felt hard — write a 3-line summary in your notes.
tasks.why: Week 1 review is the first data point — track it so week 2 has something to beat.

variants: Open MyFitnessPal and check your average daily protein for the past 5 logged days — if you're under target, identify which meal is the weakest link.
Pull your protein totals from MyFitnessPal for days 2-6, calculate the average, and write down one specific change to fix any shortfall starting tomorrow.
Review your nutrition log for this week: average protein per day, average total calories — note whether you're in the right range or need to adjust.
tasks.why: Weekly nutrition review catches drift before it becomes a habit.

variants: Take your week 1 progress photos — front, side, back — save them next to your Day 1 photos and note any visible difference, even if small.
Shoot your week 1 check-in photos right now (same 3 angles as Day 1), save them in your progress folder, and compare them side-by-side with Day 1.
Week 1 photo check: take 3 photos, same angles as Day 1, save them labeled 'Week 1', and write one sentence about what you see vs. the baseline.
tasks.why: Photo comparisons are the most honest progress tracker — start the habit of weekly documentation now.

variants: Log your weight this morning and check if it's within 1 lb of Day 1 — your surplus hasn't kicked in yet, that's normal.
Step on the scale, log the number, and compare to your Day 1 baseline — note the difference in your tracking file.
Morning weigh-in: record today's weight next to last week's number and calculate the difference.
tasks.why: Week 2 weight data starts building the trend line you'll use to calibrate calories.

variants: Pre-log your meals for today before eating anything — build the day in MyFitnessPal first, then eat what you planned.
Set up today's food log in MyFitnessPal right now, before breakfast — plan every meal to hit your protein and calorie targets.
Open MyFitnessPal and build today's eating plan from scratch before your first meal — make sure protein is distributed across at least 3 meals.
tasks.why: Planning before eating is what separates consistent trackers from inconsistent ones.

variants: Execute today's training session and apply your program's progression rule — if you completed all reps last session, add the prescribed weight today.
Train today per the program and move up in weight if your last session's log shows you hit all reps — progressive overload starts now.
Hit your workout today and check your log before loading the bar — if you completed all sets and reps last time, add weight per your program's rule.
tasks.why: Week 2 is when progressive overload begins — the log tells you what to lift, not your gut.

variants: Log all of yesterday's food in MyFitnessPal if it's not complete — check total protein and note whether you hit your target.
Finish logging Day 8's meals in MyFitnessPal and see your protein total — mark it as 'hit' or 'missed' in your notes.
Open MyFitnessPal and complete yesterday's log — check where protein ended up and note any meal where you fell short.
tasks.why: Closing out yesterday's log before starting today keeps your data accurate.

variants: Identify the one meal where you consistently undershoot protein and plan a specific fix — add a Greek yogurt, extra eggs, or a protein shake to that slot today.
Look at your last 3 days of food logs and find the meal with the lowest protein — swap one food in that meal for a higher-protein option starting today.
Find your protein weak spot this week: which meal is consistently low — fix it today by adding one high-protein food to that specific meal.
tasks.why: Fixing the one weak meal is faster than overhauling your whole diet.

variants: Do your programmed session today — before each compound lift, do 2 warm-up sets at 50% and 70% of your working weight, then hit your logged sets.
Train today and add proper warm-up sets before your main lifts — 2 sets at lighter weight to prime the movement before your working weight.
Run your workout today and include warm-up sets for every compound movement — 2 sets at reduced weight before you go to your working weight from the log.
tasks.why: Warm-up sets protect joints and improve performance on the sets that count.

variants: Log your morning weight and note your running average for the week so far — two numbers is enough to start seeing direction.
Step on the scale, log the number, and see if your weekly average is trending up from Day 1.
Morning weigh-in: log it, add it to your notes alongside previous days, and calculate a simple 3-day average.
tasks.why: Weight trends over days, not single readings — track it daily to see the real signal.

variants: Log all meals in MyFitnessPal today and make sure your protein is fully distributed — at least 30g per meal, not all at dinner.
Plan today's eating so protein is spread out: 30g+ at breakfast, 30g+ at lunch, 40g+ at dinner — log it in MyFitnessPal now.
Set up today's food log with protein distributed across 3 meals — don't let dinner carry all the weight.
tasks.why: Distributed protein intake maximizes muscle protein synthesis throughout the day.

variants: Rest day: review your Strong or Hevy log and write down every lift where you added weight this week compared to the same lift last week.
Check your workout log today and make a list of every exercise where the weight or reps went up from Week 1 — this is your progress record.
Open your training app and compare this week's weights to last week's for each lift — write down every PR, even a 2.5 lb one.
tasks.why: Documenting weekly PRs makes progress visible and fuels consistency.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal before 8am — build the full day's eating plan so protein is locked in.
Open MyFitnessPal first thing and plan all three meals for today — make sure protein hits your target before you commit to the plan.
Log your full day of eating in MyFitnessPal before you eat anything — plan first, eat second.
tasks.why: Morning planning prevents protein shortfalls that happen when you improvise all day.

variants: Do your workout today and focus specifically on bar path and tempo — slow the eccentric (lowering phase) to a 2-count on every rep of your main lifts.
Train today with one intentional tweak: on every compound lift, lower the weight in 2 seconds — don't drop it.
Execute today's session and add a 2-second lowering phase on bench, squat, and deadlift — control beats rushing.
tasks.why: Controlled eccentrics build more muscle and reinforce proper movement patterns.

variants: After your session, spend 5 minutes stretching the muscle groups you just worked — hold each stretch for 30 seconds, 2 rounds per muscle.
Post-workout: hit 4 stretches targeting today's trained muscles, 30 seconds each — do them before you leave the gym or training space.
Cool down properly after today's session: 5 minutes of targeted stretching, 30 seconds per stretch, for every major muscle you trained.
tasks.why: Regular post-workout stretching reduces soreness and keeps training frequency high.

variants: Log your weight this morning and add it to your weekly tracking — you should have at least 5-6 data points by now.
Step on the scale and log it — by end of this week you'll have enough data to calculate a true weekly average.
Morning weigh-in and log it — check if the trend is flat, up, or down compared to your Day 1 baseline.
tasks.why: Consistent daily weighing gives you the data needed to calibrate your calorie surplus.

variants: Identify one grocery item you need more of to hit protein daily — add it to your shopping list and buy it today or order it online.
Open your fridge, assess your protein sources, and restock whatever is running low — place the order or make the grocery run today.
Check your protein foods at home right now — if anything is low, add it to a shopping list and get it today before you're in a deficit tomorrow.
tasks.why: Running out of protein foods is the #1 reason people miss their targets mid-week.

variants: Execute your training session today, apply your progression rule for any lift you hit all reps on last time, and log everything in Strong or Hevy.
Train today per the program — check your last session's log before loading weights so you know exactly what to add — and record every set.
Hit your workout and use your log to determine every weight: if you completed all reps last session, move up per the program's rule.
tasks.why: The log drives the program — check it before you lift, not after.

variants: Log your weight and close out yesterday's food log in MyFitnessPal — two 2-minute habits that keep your data clean.
Morning routine: step on scale, log it, then check if yesterday's food log is complete in MyFitnessPal.
Quick data check: log today's weight and finish any incomplete meals from yesterday in MyFitnessPal.
tasks.why: Keeping data current takes 2 minutes and is what separates trackers from guessers.

variants: Plan tomorrow's workout in Strong or Hevy right now — confirm the weights you'll use based on today's log and set your targets for each lift.
Open your training app and prepare tomorrow's session: check last time's weights, apply the progression rule where needed, and set the session up.
Set up tomorrow's workout in your logging app tonight — plug in the weights based on your last session so there's zero friction when you train.
tasks.why: Prepared sessions remove the mental overhead that leads to sloppy training.

variants: Take a rest-day walk for at least 10 minutes and use the time to listen to one podcast episode about training or nutrition — no screens, just audio.
Active recovery today: 10-minute walk while listening to one fitness or nutrition podcast — absorb one actionable idea.
Rest day movement: get outside for a 10-minute walk and put on a training or nutrition podcast — low effort, high return.
tasks.why: Active recovery keeps blood flowing and keeps your mindset in the game on rest days.

variants: Review your training log for the full past 2 weeks — list every lift and note the starting weight vs. current weight for each.
Open Strong or Hevy and compare Week 1 weights to Week 2 weights for every lift — write 'up', 'same', or 'down' next to each exercise.
Two-week training review: pull up your log, identify every lift where weight increased, and write the total added pounds for your top 3 lifts.
tasks.why: Two weeks of data reveals which lifts are progressing and which need attention.

variants: Check your 2-week average protein in MyFitnessPal — calculate the daily average, compare to your target, and write one specific adjustment for week 3.
Pull your nutrition data from the past 14 days in MyFitnessPal — what's your average daily protein? What's your average calorie total? Write both numbers down.
Two-week nutrition audit: look at every logged day in MyFitnessPal, find your average daily protein and calories, and rate yourself: on track, above, or below target.
tasks.why: Two weeks is enough data to see if your nutrition plan is actually working.

variants: Take your 2-week progress photos — front, side, back — compare them to Day 1 and Week 1, then weigh yourself and write whether your weight trend is pointing up.
Week 2 check-in: photos from all 3 angles, morning weigh-in, and a 3-sentence written summary of what's working and what needs to change in week 3.
Full 2-week assessment: take progress photos, log your weight, compare to your Day 1 baseline, and write one concrete adjustment you're making going into week 3.
tasks.why: Every two weeks you stop and assess — this is how programs improve instead of stagnating.

variants: Log your weight and note whether your 2-week average trend is moving in the right direction — up slightly for muscle gain.
Step on the scale, record the number, and see if your running average is climbing at roughly 0.5 lb per week.
Morning weigh-in and log it — by now you should be seeing the start of an upward trend if your calories are dialed in.
tasks.why: 0.5-1 lb per week is the target gain rate — your scale data tells you if calories are right.

variants: Adjust your calorie target if your 2-week trend shows less than 0.5 lb gained — add 100-150 calories per day and update your MyFitnessPal goal.
Based on your 2-week weigh-in data, decide if you need to eat more — if you've gained less than 0.5 lb, bump daily calories by 150 and log the new target.
Do a calorie check: if your weight hasn't moved up in 2 weeks, increase your daily food intake by 150 calories and update your MyFitnessPal settings now.
tasks.why: Calories need active management — the scale tells you whether to eat more or hold.

variants: Execute today's training session, apply progression on every lift where you hit all reps last time, and log every set immediately after completing it.
Train today and check your log before each exercise — anywhere you hit all reps last session, add the prescribed weight today.
Hit your session today, use your log as your guide for weights, and apply progression on any lift that qualifies per your program's rule.
tasks.why: Week 3 is when progressive overload starts to feel real — your weights should be meaningfully higher than Day 1.

variants: Pre-log today's full menu in MyFitnessPal before eating — make sure protein hits your target and calories are in surplus range.
Build today's meal plan in MyFitnessPal now, before breakfast — every meal, every gram — so hitting your numbers is automatic.
Open MyFitnessPal and pre-load today's eating plan with all three meals before you eat anything — lock in the protein target first.
tasks.why: Pre-planning food is a skill — the faster you build it, the easier every day gets.

variants: Do your workout today and time your rest periods — use a timer app to keep rests at 2-3 minutes between compound sets and 60-90 seconds between accessory sets.
Train today and enforce your rest periods with a phone timer — 2-3 minutes after squats and deadlifts, 90 seconds after accessories.
Use a timer during today's session: 2 minutes rest after compound lifts, 90 seconds after isolation work — stick to it the whole session.
tasks.why: Consistent rest periods control training density and keep volume manageable.

variants: After your session, write a 3-sentence training note in your phone: what felt strong, what felt off, and one thing to focus on next session.
Post-workout log entry: open your notes app and write what went well today, what struggled, and your specific goal for next session.
Before you leave the gym, write a quick session debrief — 3 sentences max: wins, struggles, and one cue to carry into the next workout.
tasks.why: Session notes create a feedback loop that makes every workout smarter than the last.

variants: Log your weight and check if you're averaging 0.5-1 lb per week gain — if you gained more than 1 lb this week, consider holding calories steady.
Morning scale check and log — compare to your week 2 average and see if the trend is in the 0.5-1 lb per week range.
Step on scale, record it, and evaluate: too fast means too many calories, too slow means too few — what does your trend say?
tasks.why: Gaining faster than 1 lb/week adds unnecessary fat — the scale is your feedback system.

variants: Identify the compound lift you're weakest on right now and watch one specific technique video for it — under 5 minutes, save one cue to your notes.
Find your weakest main lift from your log this week, search for a 3-5 minute form tutorial, and write one thing to change in your next session.
Pick the lift where you've progressed slowest, find a short technique video for it, and add one form cue to your session notes for next time.
tasks.why: Technique is a hidden lever for progression — better form unlocks weight you couldn't lift before.

variants: Rest day: meal prep one high-protein food for the next 3 days — cook a batch of chicken, hard-boil a dozen eggs, or prep Greek yogurt portions.
Active recovery and protein prep: cook or portion out one high-protein food source today that covers 3 days of meals.
Rest day food prep: spend 10 minutes batch cooking or portioning one protein source — eggs, chicken, tuna packets — so you're covered through Wednesday.
tasks.why: Prepped protein means you hit your target even on the laziest days.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal and confirm your protein target is achievable before you eat — adjust if needed.
Open MyFitnessPal and plan today's food first — every meal pre-logged before you eat the first bite.
Build today's food plan in MyFitnessPal right now so your protein goal is locked in from the start.
tasks.why: Day 18 pre-logging should feel automatic — if it doesn't, make it a non-negotiable morning task.

variants: Execute today's training session with the technique cue you wrote down yesterday — apply it on every rep of the lift you targeted.
Train today and actively apply the form cue you identified for your weak lift — think about it on every single rep, not just the first.
Use today's session to implement the technique adjustment from your notes — apply the cue on every rep and note whether it changes how the lift feels.
tasks.why: Deliberate practice of one cue at a time is how technique improves.

variants: Log every set of today's workout in Strong or Hevy in real time — don't wait until after the session to enter weights.
Record each set immediately after completing it today — open the app between sets and log weight, reps, and notes before moving on.
Live-log your workout today: enter each set's weight and reps into Strong or Hevy the moment you finish the set, not at the end.
tasks.why: Real-time logging is more accurate and keeps you accountable between sets.

variants: Log your weight and track your weekly average — are you hitting the 0.5-1 lb per week gain target?
Morning weigh-in: log the number and calculate your running weekly average for this week.
Step on scale, record it, and compare to yesterday — this week's average should tell you whether to eat more, less, or stay.
tasks.why: Frequent weighing combined with weekly averaging gives you the most accurate trend signal.

variants: Review the past 3 sessions in your training log — identify any lift that hasn't progressed in 2+ sessions and think about why.
Open Strong or Hevy and look at your last 3 workouts — flag any exercise where weight or reps have stalled for 2 sessions in a row.
Check your training log for stalls: find any lift that hasn't gone up in 2 sessions and write a one-line theory for why — sleep, calories, technique?
tasks.why: Catching stalls early lets you address them before they become frustrating plateaus.

variants: Do your programmed session today and on the lift where you've stalled, attempt one more rep than last time before adding weight.
Train today and specifically push your stalled lift — try to beat your last reps by 1 before moving to heavier weight.
Execute today's workout and test your stalled lift: get one extra rep at your current weight before deciding whether to increase.
tasks.why: Squeezing every rep before adding weight ensures you're truly ready for progression.

variants: Log your weight and do a quick gut check — how is sleep this week? Under 7 hours is the most common cause of stalled lifts.
Morning weigh-in: log it, then honestly rate your sleep quality this week from 1-5 in your notes.
Step on scale and log it — then write down how many hours you slept last night, because sleep drives 80% of muscle recovery.
tasks.why: Sleep is a training variable — tracking it helps explain weight and strength fluctuations.

variants: Set a consistent bedtime alarm for the next 7 days — pick a time that gives you 7.5-8 hours and stick to it without exceptions.
Establish a bedtime right now: pick a time, set a recurring alarm, and commit to it for the next week — sleep is recovery.
Add a 'time to sleep' alarm to your phone for the next 7 nights — 7.5-8 hours before your wake time — and treat it like a training session.
tasks.why: Consistent sleep timing improves recovery, strength output, and muscle growth.

variants: Rest day: go for a 15-minute walk, drink an extra liter of water, and make sure dinner tonight hits at least 40g of protein.
Active recovery: 15-minute walk, full water intake for the day, and a high-protein dinner — check all 3 boxes before bed.
Rest day checklist: walk 15 minutes, hit your daily water target, and eat at least 40g of protein at dinner — log all three in your notes.
tasks.why: Recovery days done right accelerate gains — they're not optional rest, they're active preparation.

variants: Three-week review: open your training log and count how many total sessions you've completed — write 'X of Y planned sessions done' in your notes.
Log your morning weight, then do a 3-week audit: how many workouts completed, how many missed, and what's your excuse for any misses?
Pull up your workout app and count your sessions for weeks 1-3 — write the number and compare it to your planned schedule.
tasks.why: Session completion rate is the most honest metric of consistency in the first month.

variants: Take your 3-week progress photos — front, side, back — compare them to Day 1 and write 3 sentences about what you see changing.
Week 3 photo check: shoot all 3 angles, put them next to Day 1 photos, and write down any visible change in shape, fullness, or definition.
3-week check-in photos from the same 3 angles as Day 1, saved and labeled 'Week 3' — write one honest sentence about the visual progress.
tasks.why: Three weeks is when the first subtle visual changes begin — document them before they grow and you forget the starting point.

variants: Review your Day 1 starting weights in Strong or Hevy and compare them to today's — write down the total weight added across your top 4 lifts in 3 weeks.
Open your training log and pull your week 1 Day 1 weights for bench, squat, deadlift, and overhead press — how much have you added in 3 weeks? Write it out.
Strength progress check: find the starting weight for each of your main lifts and calculate how many pounds you've added in 21 days — list each lift.
tasks.why: Tracking strength numbers is more motivating than scale weight — it shows the program is working.

variants: Log your weight and compare it to your Day 1 baseline — after 3 weeks, you should be up 1-3 lbs if your calories are right.
Morning weigh-in: log it and calculate the difference from your Day 1 weight recorded 3 weeks ago.
Step on scale and record it — compare to Day 1 and note whether you're in the 1-3 lb gain range after 3 weeks.
tasks.why: Three weeks in, your weight trend should show a clear direction — up by 1-3 lbs is ideal progress.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal and specifically plan a pre-workout meal with 30-40g of protein 1-2 hours before training.
Build today's food plan and schedule a pre-workout meal: 30-40g protein, some carbs, 1.5 hours before your session — log it now.
Set up today's nutrition in MyFitnessPal and include a proper pre-workout meal — protein + carbs, logged 1-2 hours before you train.
tasks.why: Pre-workout nutrition improves performance and reduces muscle breakdown during training.

variants: Execute today's training session and try to beat your Day 10 rep count by at least 2 reps on your primary lift — check your log for that number.
Train today and specifically challenge yourself on the main lift — reference your Day 10 log and add 2 more reps or 5 more pounds compared to that session.
Hit your workout today with a specific target: beat your Day 10 performance on your strongest compound lift — more weight or more reps, not both.
tasks.why: Callbacking to Day 10 gives you a concrete benchmark that reveals 12 days of real progress.

variants: Meal prep today: cook a batch of protein for the next 2-3 days — chicken, ground turkey, or eggs — and portion it into containers.
Spend 10 minutes prepping a protein source you can grab over the next 3 days — batch cook now so hitting your target is effortless.
Batch prep one protein food right now — cook it, portion it, refrigerate it — so 3 days of protein isn't a daily decision.
tasks.why: Prepped food removes the friction that causes people to miss their protein targets mid-week.

variants: Today's session: after your final set of each compound lift, write your RPE (rate of perceived exertion, 1-10) in Strong or Hevy's notes field.
Train today and add an RPE note to every compound lift after its final set — this data helps you calibrate intensity going forward.
Execute your workout and log your effort level (RPE 1-10) for each main lift's final set — you'll use this data to know when to push harder.
tasks.why: RPE data reveals when you're under-training (consistently RPE 6) or overreaching (consistently RPE 10).

variants: After training, log your full post-workout meal in MyFitnessPal — at least 30-40g protein within 30 minutes of finishing your session.
Post-workout nutrition: eat and log 30-40g protein within 30 minutes of finishing today's session — write it in MyFitnessPal immediately.
Close out today's training with a logged post-workout meal: 30g+ protein, logged in MyFitnessPal before you shower.
tasks.why: Post-workout protein accelerates recovery — log it to make the habit stick.

variants: Log your weight and check your weekly average — if it's flat for 2 consecutive weeks, your calories need to go up by 150.
Morning scale check and log — has your weight trend stalled for more than 10 days? If so, increase daily calories starting today.
Weigh in and evaluate: two weeks with no upward movement on the scale means bump calories 150 per day — what does your trend show?
tasks.why: A flat scale trend means your body has adapted — more calories are required to drive growth.

variants: Find a new high-protein recipe online — something that uses an ingredient you already have — and save it to cook this week.
Search 'high protein meal 40g+' and pick one recipe you can make with ingredients you already own — bookmark or screenshot it.
Expand your protein menu: find one new recipe with 40g+ protein per serving, save it, and plan to make it in the next 3 days.
tasks.why: Food variety keeps tracking sustainable long-term — eating the same 3 meals causes compliance to break down.

variants: Rest day: take a 15-minute walk and review your workout log — pick the one lift where your numbers have grown the most and screenshot it.
Active recovery: 15-minute walk plus 5 minutes reviewing your training log — identify your biggest strength gain so far and write the numbers down.
Rest day: do 15 minutes of light movement and spend 5 minutes in your training app looking at your best progression — screenshot the chart.
tasks.why: Seeing your fastest-progressing lift keeps motivation high during recovery days.

variants: Pre-log today's full meals in MyFitnessPal — include your pre-workout and post-workout nutrition in the plan.
Build today's eating schedule in MyFitnessPal: breakfast, pre-workout meal, post-workout protein, dinner — all logged before you start eating.
Set up today's food log now: pre-workout meal, post-workout protein, and two other meals — make sure protein is distributed across all of them.
tasks.why: Day 25 nutrition planning should be a locked routine, not a daily negotiation.

variants: Execute today's session and on your biggest compound lift, try to hit a weight you've never lifted before — even if it's 5 lbs more than last time.
Train today with one goal: set a personal record on your primary lift, even by a single rep or 2.5 lbs — log it in Strong or Hevy as a PR.
Hit your workout today and chase a small PR on your main compound movement — 1 more rep or 5 more pounds than your previous best.
tasks.why: Small PRs compound — setting one every week adds up to massive strength gains by day 90.

variants: After today's session, look up 'compound lift accessory exercises' for your weakest muscle group and write down 2 accessory movements to add later.
Post-workout: identify your physically weakest muscle group from today's session and research 2 accessory exercises that target it — save them for week 5.
Train, then research: after your session, find 2 accessory exercises for the muscle that felt weakest today — write them in your notes for future use.
tasks.why: Accessory work planned now is ready to add in week 5 when the program calls for it.

variants: Log your weight and note any soreness — excessive soreness means recovery isn't keeping up, which kills progressive overload.
Morning weigh-in: log the number and rate your soreness from yesterday's training on a 1-5 scale in your notes.
Step on scale, record it, and check in on how your body feels — write 'fresh', 'moderate', or 'wrecked' next to today's weight.
tasks.why: Soreness tracking tells you if recovery (sleep, protein, calories) is adequate for the training volume.

variants: Pre-log meals for today and bump protein by 5-10g above target — slightly higher protein on heavy training days accelerates recovery.
Add an extra 5-10g of protein to today's food plan in MyFitnessPal — a small upgrade on training days that adds up over the week.
Build today's meal plan with a slight protein bump — add one extra protein serving to your usual plan and log it in MyFitnessPal.
tasks.why: Slightly higher protein on training days provides extra amino acids when muscles need them most.

variants: Today's session: warmup properly, then focus on full range of motion on every rep — touch chest on bench, squat to parallel or below, full lockout on deadlift.
Train today with a full range of motion focus — partial reps don't build as much muscle, so commit to the full movement on every single rep.
Execute your workout and be strict about ROM: full depth on squats, chest touch on bench, lockout on press and deadlift — no shortcuts.
tasks.why: Full range of motion builds more muscle than partial reps — it's free gains you're leaving on the table if you skip it.

variants: Log your weight and check this week's trend — if you've gained more than 1 lb this week alone, reduce calories by 100 tomorrow.
Morning weigh-in: log it and compare to your Monday weight — too fast a gain means cut back slightly, flat means add more.
Weigh in and evaluate the week's trend: 0.5-1 lb total gain for the week is the target — adjust calories if you're outside that range.
tasks.why: Active calorie management keeps lean gains on track — don't set and forget your surplus.

variants: Write down your 5 most-eaten high-protein foods this week in your notes — these are your protein staples and you should always have them stocked.
Make a list of the protein foods you've actually eaten in the past 7 days — identify your top 5 and make sure they're on your next grocery run.
Review your MyFitnessPal log for this week and find the 5 foods contributing most to your protein total — note them as your core protein sources.
tasks.why: Knowing your protein staples makes meal prep automatic and shopping efficient.

variants: Rest day: do 10 minutes of mobility work focused on your hips and shoulders — search 'hip flexor stretch' and 'shoulder mobility routine' and do both.
Active recovery: spend 10 minutes on mobility — hip flexors, thoracic spine, and shoulder capsule — using a short YouTube routine.
Mobility work today: 10 minutes targeting your tightest areas from training — hips if you squat, shoulders if you press — find a quick routine and do it.
tasks.why: Mobility work on rest days prevents tightness that degrades technique on main lifts.

variants: Four-week training review: open your log and list how many sessions you completed in each of the 4 weeks — note any week with fewer than planned.
Month 1 review: pull up Strong or Hevy, count sessions per week for all 4 weeks, and write your total sessions completed vs. planned.
End of week 4: count your completed training sessions across all 4 weeks and calculate your consistency percentage — sessions done divided by sessions planned.
tasks.why: Month 1 consistency data is the clearest predictor of whether you'll still be training at day 90.

variants: Take your 4-week progress photos — same 3 angles — and line them up next to Day 1, Week 1, and Week 2 photos to see the full progression.
Week 4 photo check: shoot all 3 angles, put them in your progress folder, and compare the full timeline from Day 1 — write one observation about each angle.
Shoot your 4-week progress photos from all 3 angles, compare them to your Day 1 baseline, and write a 3-sentence assessment of visible changes.
tasks.why: Monthly photo comparisons reveal progress that daily mirrors completely miss.

variants: Month 1 strength audit: look up your starting weights for bench, squat, deadlift, and overhead press from week 1 and list the current weights — calculate total added pounds.
Pull your Day 1 starting weights for every main lift from your log and compare them to your most recent session — write the progression for each exercise.
Strength progress report for month 1: find your first session's weights and today's weights for every main compound lift — calculate and record the gains.
tasks.why: One month of strength data shows you whether the program is working and whether progressive overload has been real.

variants: Log your weight and compare to your 4-week average — you should be 2-4 lbs above Day 1 if calories and training have been consistent.
Morning weigh-in: log it and calculate your total weight gain since Day 1 — the target is 2-4 lbs in the first month.
Step on scale and record it — 4 weeks in, your weight should be 2-4 lbs above your Day 1 number if the program is working.
tasks.why: 2-4 lbs in the first month is healthy lean mass gain — more than that is mostly fat, less means calories need adjusting.

variants: Set your nutrition targets for month 2 in MyFitnessPal — adjust protein goal if your weight has changed by more than 3 lbs (recalculate from your current weight).
Update your protein target for month 2: recalculate bodyweight × 0.8 using your current weight and adjust your MyFitnessPal goal if it's changed.
Recalibrate your nutrition targets: step on scale, multiply current weight by 0.8 for protein grams, and update MyFitnessPal to reflect month 2 targets.
tasks.why: As your bodyweight increases, your protein target should increase with it.

variants: Do your last session of month 1 — make it count by attempting a PR on your best lift before you walk out.
Train today and chase a personal record on your top compound lift — set a meaningful number to beat heading into month 2.
Close out month 1 with a strong session and at least one attempted PR — log it in Strong or Hevy with a 'Month 1 Final' note.
tasks.why: A strong final session of the month builds momentum and gives you a clear benchmark heading into month 2.

variants: Month 1 milestone: log your weight, take your photos, and write your 3 most significant wins from the past 30 days in your notes.
Day 30 checkpoint: morning weigh-in, 3 progress photos from all angles, and a written list of your top 3 achievements this month.
30-day mark: document your current state — weight, photos, and a written record of the 3 things that went best — before starting month 2.
tasks.why: Documenting month 1 achievements locks in the progress and sets the bar for month 2.

variants: Write your month 2 intention in your notes: one specific strength goal, one specific nutrition goal, and one training habit to add or fix.
Set 3 month 2 targets right now — a specific lift to PR, a weekly protein hit rate goal, and one recovery habit to lock in — write them somewhere you'll see them.
Before month 2 begins, define your 3 targets: what weight you want to hit on your main lift by day 60, your average weekly protein target, and one habit to build.
tasks.why: Written goals for month 2 replace vague motivation with concrete targets that drive decisions.

variants: Calculate your consistency score for month 1: sessions completed ÷ sessions planned × 100 — and set a target of equal or higher for month 2, written in your notes.
Month 1 final review: consistency rate, weight trend, strength progress on your top 4 lifts — write a 5-sentence summary and set one specific improvement for month 2.
Day 30 full assessment: compile your session count, weight change, protein hit rate, and strength gains into one written summary — this is your month 1 report card.
tasks.why: A full month 1 audit gives you the data to make month 2 more effective — the score improves every month you do this.

variants: Month 2 starts today — log your weight, open your workout app, and confirm your program's next session is ready to go.
First day of month 2: morning weigh-in, log it, then open Strong or Hevy and verify your next session is set up with correct weights.
Kick off month 2 right: log your weight, pull up your training app, and confirm the weights in your next session reflect last month's progress.
tasks.why: Month 2 is about progressive overload — every session's weights must be informed by the log.

variants: Add one accessory exercise to today's session — choose from the 2 you wrote down on Day 25 — and do 3 sets of 10-12 reps after your main lifts.
Incorporate your first accessory movement today: pick one from your Day 25 notes, add 3 sets of 10-12 after your compound work, and log it.
Today's workout gets an upgrade: add one of the accessory exercises from your notes (from Day 25) after your main lifts — 3 sets, 10-12 reps, logged.
tasks.why: Month 2 adds accessory work to target weak points — start with one exercise before layering more.

variants: Execute your full session with the new accessory included — log everything including the accessory sets and note how the new movement felt.
Run your complete workout today — main compounds plus the accessory you added — and record every set in Strong or Hevy including the new exercise.
Train today per the program plus your new accessory movement — log it all and write one sentence about how the accessory targeted your weak point.
tasks.why: Adding accessory volume strategically is how month 2 builds on month 1's foundation.

variants: Log your weight and check this week's calorie average in MyFitnessPal — is it landing in your target surplus range?
Morning weigh-in and a quick check of this week's logged calories in MyFitnessPal — on target, high, or low?
Step on scale, record it, then open MyFitnessPal and verify your calorie average for the week so far.
tasks.why: Calorie tracking drift happens slowly — weekly checks catch it before it derails progress.

variants: Take your monthly measurements today: arms, chest, and waist — write the numbers in your notes next to your Day 1 measurements.
Pull out a tape measure and record your arm (flexed), chest, and waist circumferences — log them next to your month 1 baseline numbers.
Monthly body measurement check: arms flexed, chest at nipple line, waist at navel — write all three numbers and compare to your starting point.
tasks.why: Tape measurements capture muscle growth that scale weight alone can't show.

variants: Rest day: spend 6 minutes reading your chosen program's FAQ or troubleshooting section for tips on breaking plateaus — write one new strategy down.
Active recovery: review your program's documentation for plateau-busting techniques — note one method you haven't tried and save it for when you need it.
Rest day reading: find your program's advice on stalled lifts — read it, write down one technique to deploy if a lift stops progressing for 3 sessions.
tasks.why: Knowing plateau strategies before you need them means faster fixes when stalls happen.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal and make sure protein is front-loaded — at least 30g at breakfast.
Log today's food plan before eating — specifically ensure breakfast has 30g+ protein so you start the day ahead.
Build today's meals in MyFitnessPal now and put 30g of protein in breakfast — front-loading protein is easier than chasing it at night.
tasks.why: Front-loading protein makes hitting daily targets almost automatic.

variants: Execute today's session and on every compound lift, add weight per your program's progression rule — no skipping the increase if you hit all reps.
Train today and be disciplined about adding weight where the program calls for it — check your log before each lift and apply the rule.
Hit your workout and use your log to drive every weight decision — if last session shows completed reps, today you add weight. No exceptions.
tasks.why: Month 2 progressive overload is non-negotiable — the rule exists so you don't talk yourself out of adding weight.

variants: After training, write a quick progress note comparing today's weights to your Day 1 starting weights for those same lifts — calculate total pounds added.
Post-session: open your notes and calculate how much total weight you've added across your main lifts since Day 1 — write it out lift by lift.
After your session, do a quick audit: your day 1 weight vs. today's weight on bench, squat, deadlift, press — list the gains for each.
tasks.why: Tracking cumulative strength gains makes the work feel worthwhile and drives continued effort.

variants: Log your weight and check your weekly average — if flat for the past 2 weeks, bump daily calories by 150 starting today.
Morning weigh-in: compare your weekly average to the previous week — no movement means add 150 calories to your daily target.
Weigh in, log it, and evaluate the trend: two flat weeks means your body has adapted and needs more fuel — adjust calories upward if that's the case.
tasks.why: Flat weight for 2 weeks means your metabolism has caught up — add calories to restart progress.

variants: Film a 10-second video of your squat from the side — watch it and note one thing that looks different from proper form you've seen in tutorials.
Record a short clip of yourself doing 3 squats from a side angle — watch it back and write down one form issue to fix next session.
Set up your phone to record your squat from the side, do 3 reps, then watch it and identify one technical issue to address.
tasks.why: Form video check in month 2 catches habits that feel right but look wrong — fix them before they become ingrained.

variants: Run your full training session today and apply the form correction you identified from the video — focus on that cue on every rep of every squat set.
Train today with your squat form fix as the priority — apply the correction from your video review on every single rep and note whether it changes how it feels.
Execute today's workout with your video-identified form cue as the main focus — apply it deliberately on every squat rep and log your observation after.
tasks.why: Deliberate technique work during the session locks in corrections faster than passive awareness.

variants: Week 5 review: open your training log and count this week's sessions — also list the 3 lifts where weight went up this week.
Five-week mark: review your workout log and note which compound lifts progressed this week and which stayed flat.
Weekly training review: pull up Strong or Hevy and identify every lift where you added weight or reps this week vs. last week.
tasks.why: Weekly lift-by-lift review catches stalls while you can still address them before they compound.

variants: Take your 5-week progress photos — 3 angles — and line them up with Week 4 and Day 1 to see the full progression arc.
Week 5 photo check: shoot front, side, and back, save them, and compare the full sequence from Day 1 through today.
Shoot your 5-week photos from all 3 angles and write one sentence per angle about visible change compared to your Day 1 baseline.
tasks.why: Photo comparisons at 5 weeks reveal the real changes that mirrors and scale alone can't show.

variants: Review this week's nutrition data in MyFitnessPal: average protein, average calories, and days you hit or missed protein target — write a 3-line summary.
Pull your week 5 nutrition data from MyFitnessPal — daily protein averages, calorie averages — and rate this week compared to week 4.
Nutrition audit for week 5: check every logged day, calculate average protein and calories, and identify the one day or meal where you consistently fall short.
tasks.why: Five weeks of data reveals patterns — both good habits to reinforce and bad ones to fix.

variants: Log your weight and compare to last week's average — you should be up 0.5-1 lb per week for clean lean gains.
Morning weigh-in: is the weekly trend still in the 0.5-1 lb range? Log it and assess.
Step on scale, log it, and check the trend — 0.5-1 lb per week is the target, more means cut back calories slightly.
tasks.why: Maintaining the right gain rate prevents excessive fat accumulation while building muscle.

variants: Film a 10-second video of your bench press from the side — watch it and note whether your bar path is straight, your arch is consistent, and your elbows are at the right angle.
Record 3 reps of your bench from a side angle — watch the clip and identify one technical issue compared to your understanding of proper form.
Set up your phone to record your bench press, shoot 3 reps, watch the video, and write one specific form cue to apply next session.
tasks.why: Bench form video in week 5 catches bar path and elbow angle issues before they limit your max.

variants: Execute today's full training session and add your second accessory exercise — choose from your Day 25 notes and add 3 sets of 10-12 after your compounds.
Train today and introduce the second accessory exercise from your list — 3 sets of 10-12 reps, logged in Strong or Hevy after the main work.
Add your second accessory movement to today's session — pick it from your notes, do 3 sets of 10-12 after compounds, and log it.
tasks.why: Progressive volume increase through accessories builds the detail and weak-point strength that compounds alone can't reach.

variants: Log your weight and note your sleep hours last night — if under 7 hours, prioritize an earlier bedtime tonight.
Morning weigh-in: log the number and check last night's sleep — was it 7+ hours? Note it in your tracking log.
Step on scale, record it, and honestly note how many hours you slept — sleep debt shows up as flat strength and slow recovery.
tasks.why: Sleep is the most underrated muscle-building variable — tracking it reveals its impact on training quality.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal, including a pre-workout and post-workout meal, and confirm protein hits your target.
Plan today's eating in MyFitnessPal with specific attention to the meals around your training window — pre and post-workout logged.
Build today's full food plan in MyFitnessPal before eating — make sure you have a planned pre-workout and post-workout protein source.
tasks.why: Peri-workout nutrition planning in month 2 should be standard — it directly impacts training performance.

variants: Rest day: take a 15-minute walk and spend the last 5 minutes doing breathing exercises — 4-count inhale, 4-count hold, 4-count exhale — for recovery and stress management.
Active recovery: 15-minute walk plus 5 minutes of slow diaphragmatic breathing — this directly improves recovery quality and sleep.
Rest day routine: walk for 15 minutes, then do 5 rounds of box breathing (4-4-4-4 count) — both support recovery and next session's performance.
tasks.why: Parasympathetic activation through breathing and walking accelerates muscle recovery between sessions.

variants: Log your weight, then open your training app and verify your next session's weights are correctly updated from your last session.
Morning weigh-in and session prep: log your weight, then pull up Strong or Hevy and confirm today's workout has current, not outdated, weights.
Step on scale, log it, then check your training app — make sure today's session weights reflect your most recent progress, not last month's.
tasks.why: Checking the app before training prevents the common mistake of lifting last week's weight instead of progressing.

variants: Execute your training session and on the lift you filmed on Day 34, apply the correction — notice if the technique change affects how the weight moves.
Train today with your Day 34 squat cue actively in mind — apply it on every rep and see if it changes your depth or bar path.
Use today's session to apply your video-identified form correction from earlier this week — be deliberate on every rep and log how it felt.
tasks.why: Technique corrections need 2-3 sessions of deliberate practice before they become automatic.

variants: After your session, plan next week's training schedule in your calendar — block every session with specific days and times.
Post-workout: open your calendar and schedule next week's training sessions with exact days, times, and which session type for each slot.
Before you leave training today, block next week's workouts in your calendar — day, time, and session name for each one.
tasks.why: Scheduling ahead is what makes 5x/week training possible without constant willpower decisions.

variants: Log your weight and compare your current 7-day average to your week 4 average — the trend should show gradual upward movement.
Morning weigh-in: calculate a 7-day average and compare it to last week's 7-day average — is the line going up?
Step on scale, log it, and pull your 7-day average — compare to the previous week's average and note the direction.
tasks.why: Seven-day averages eliminate daily noise and give you the clearest signal of whether your surplus is working.

variants: Log today's meals in MyFitnessPal and specifically track creatine intake if you're using it — 5g daily, any time, with food.
Pre-log today's food and if you've been considering creatine, add it to your supplement plan — 5g monohydrate daily, no loading phase required.
Plan today's meals in MyFitnessPal — and if creatine is in your stack, log it as a daily habit (5g/day, every day).
tasks.why: Creatine is the most research-backed supplement for strength and muscle gain — 5g daily is the dose.

variants: Run today's session and hit a rep PR on at least one lift — more reps at the same weight counts as progress if you can't add plates yet.
Execute your workout today and specifically aim for 1-2 extra reps on your second-strongest lift — rep PRs count as real progressive overload.
Train today and set a rep personal record on at least one lift — if the weight hasn't gone up, the reps must.
tasks.why: Rep PRs are valid progressive overload — they build the capacity needed to eventually add more weight.

variants: Log your weight and note whether your energy levels before and during training have been high, moderate, or low this week.
Morning weigh-in: log the number and rate your training energy this week from 1-5 — it's a direct indicator of whether calories and sleep are right.
Weigh in, record it, and honestly grade your energy in the gym this week — low energy means something in your recovery protocol needs fixing.
tasks.why: Energy levels in training are a direct readout of sleep, calories, and stress — track them to diagnose problems early.

variants: Write down your current working weight for your top 3 compound lifts and set a specific target weight for each by Day 60 — write both numbers in your notes.
Set Day 60 strength targets right now: look up today's weights for bench, squat, and deadlift and write a realistic but challenging number to hit by day 60.
In your notes, list today's weights for your 3 main lifts and set a Day 60 goal for each — ambitious but achievable based on your current progression rate.
tasks.why: Day 60 targets give the next 20 sessions a specific direction — training without targets is aimless.

variants: Rest day: take measurements — arms, chest, waist — and compare to your Day 32 measurements to see month 2's body composition changes.
Monthly measurement check: arms, chest, and waist with a tape measure — compare to your Week 5 measurements and note the differences.
Measurement day on your rest day: tape measure all 3 sites, record the numbers, and compare to last month's measurements — calculate each change.
tasks.why: Measurement comparisons in month 2 are when you start seeing real body composition changes.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal before 8am and ensure carbs are scheduled around your training window for maximum energy.
Build today's food plan and place your biggest carb servings pre- and post-workout — log it in MyFitnessPal before you eat anything.
Set up today's nutrition in MyFitnessPal and front-load carbs around training — breakfast or pre-workout and post-workout should have the most.
tasks.why: Carb timing around training fuels performance and speeds glycogen replenishment.

variants: Execute today's session and specifically work toward your Day 60 strength targets — check if you're on track based on your current progression rate.
Train today and after the session, calculate whether your current weekly weight increases will get you to your Day 60 target — are you on pace?
Hit your workout today with your Day 60 strength goal in the back of your mind — log your weights and verify you're progressing fast enough to hit it.
tasks.why: Reverse-engineering your Day 60 target from today's weight keeps progression intentional.

variants: After training, film a 10-second video of your deadlift from the side — check bar path, back angle, and lockout — write one correction to apply.
Record 3 deadlift reps from the side angle, watch the video, and identify one technique issue to fix in the next session.
Set up your phone to film your deadlift today — shoot 3 reps, review the video, and note the most important form issue you see.
tasks.why: Deadlift form check in month 2 catches rounding or bar drift before weights get heavy enough to cause injury.

variants: Week 6 review: list every lift where you progressed this week — by weight or reps — and flag any that have stalled for more than 2 sessions.
Six-week training review: open your log and do a lift-by-lift scan — weight up, same, or down vs. last week for every exercise.
Weekly audit: pull up Strong or Hevy and find every exercise that progressed vs. every one that stalled — write both lists.
tasks.why: Tracking progress vs. stalls weekly keeps the program responsive instead of mechanical.

variants: Take week 6 progress photos from all 3 angles, compare them to Day 1 and week 4, and write 2 sentences about visible muscle development.
Week 6 photo check — 3 angles, saved and labeled — then compare to your day 1 baseline and write one sentence about the biggest change you see.
Shoot your 6-week progress photos, line them up with your Day 1 photos, and write a 3-sentence honest assessment of your visual progress so far.
tasks.why: Six weeks is when visible muscle changes become undeniable — documenting now captures the inflection point.

variants: Nutrition review for week 6: pull your MyFitnessPal data, calculate your 7-day average protein and calories, and compare them to your targets — adjust if needed.
Week 6 nutrition audit: open MyFitnessPal, review every logged day this week, and calculate your average daily protein — write whether it's at, above, or below target.
Full nutrition check for week 6: average daily protein, average daily calories, and the number of days you hit your protein target — write all three numbers.
tasks.why: Six weeks of nutrition data gives you the most accurate picture of whether your diet is supporting your training.

variants: Log your weight and apply your deadlift form correction from Day 41's video in today's mental preparation — write the cue in your notes before training.
Morning weigh-in: log it, then write down the deadlift technique cue from your Day 41 video review — you'll use it in every pull today.
Step on scale, log it, then pull up your Day 41 notes and find the deadlift correction — remind yourself of it before today's session.
tasks.why: Technique corrections need intentional recall before each session to stick.

variants: Pre-log today's meals and add a new high-protein food you haven't eaten this week — variety prevents diet fatigue in month 2.
Set up today's food plan in MyFitnessPal and include at least one protein source you haven't eaten in the past 3 days.
Build today's meals in MyFitnessPal and deliberately rotate in a different protein source — eat what you haven't had in a few days.
tasks.why: Protein variety prevents dietary monotony which is what causes tracking compliance to drop.

variants: Execute today's training session and apply your deadlift technique correction on every rep — after your final set, note whether it changed the lift.
Train today and use the deadlift form cue from your video review on every single pull — log the session and add a note about how the technique felt.
Run your full workout today with deliberate deadlift technique focus — apply your correction and write one post-session note about the improvement.
tasks.why: Three weeks of technique work before weights get truly heavy builds the movement pattern that keeps you safe.

variants: Log your weight and verify your weekly average is still trending upward — if flat, add 100 calories per day and update MyFitnessPal.
Morning weigh-in: is the trend still moving? Compare to 2 weeks ago — flat trend means add calories, rising trend means hold steady.
Step on scale and log it — compare today's weekly average to 2 weeks ago; if growth has stalled, bump calories by 100 per day.
tasks.why: Calorie adjustments every 2 weeks keep lean gains on track as your metabolism adapts.

variants: Film your overhead press today from the front — check that your bar path is vertical, your elbows are under the bar, and you're not overarching your lower back.
Record 3 overhead press reps from the front angle, watch the video, and identify one issue to correct in the next session.
Set up your phone to film your overhead press from the front — shoot 3 working reps, watch the clip, and write one technique note.
tasks.why: Overhead press is the most technically demanding pressing movement — catching issues early prevents shoulder problems.

variants: Execute today's full session including all accessories — log every set, note any PR, and apply your overhead press correction from the video.
Run your complete workout today, apply your overhead press technique correction on every rep, and log every set in Strong or Hevy.
Train today per the program, apply the press form cue from your video check, and record all sets — note whether the correction changes how the lift feels.
tasks.why: Consistent technique improvement across all four main lifts is the defining work of month 2.

variants: Log your weight and calculate your midpoint progress: you're halfway through the 90 days — how many pounds have you gained since Day 1?
Morning weigh-in at the midpoint: log it and calculate the total weight change from Day 1 — you're halfway there.
Step on scale, record it, and do the math: current weight minus Day 1 weight equals your 45-day progress number.
tasks.why: Day 45 is the program midpoint — knowing your progress number tells you exactly how the second half should be calibrated.

variants: Write a midpoint self-assessment in your notes: what's working in training, what's working in nutrition, and one thing you'll fix for the second half.
Midpoint review: write 3 sentences — one about training consistency, one about nutrition compliance, one about what you're changing for days 46-90.
Day 45 audit: rate yourself on training (1-10), nutrition (1-10), and sleep (1-10), then identify the one lowest-scored area to prioritize.
tasks.why: Midpoint self-assessment with honest ratings guides where to focus energy for the second half.

variants: Rest day: walk 15 minutes and update your Day 60 strength targets based on your current progression rate — are the targets still realistic?
Active recovery and goal calibration: 15-minute walk, then revisit your Day 60 strength targets — are you on track, ahead, or behind? Adjust if needed.
Rest day: 15-minute walk plus a 5-minute check on your Day 60 targets — look at your current progression rate and confirm you'll hit them.
tasks.why: Midpoint target calibration ensures your goals are driving the right training decisions.

variants: Pre-log today's meals and check that your protein is distributed across at least 4 servings — spreading it out maximizes muscle protein synthesis.
Build today's food plan in MyFitnessPal with protein spread across 4+ eating occasions — never let one meal carry more than 40% of the daily total.
Set up today's nutrition in MyFitnessPal and distribute protein across breakfast, lunch, an afternoon snack, and dinner — log it before eating.
tasks.why: Four protein feedings per day maximizes muscle protein synthesis compared to front- or back-loading.

variants: Execute today's session and time your rest periods strictly — 2.5 minutes between compound sets with a phone timer, no longer, no shorter.
Train today with exact rest intervals — set a 2.5-minute timer after every compound set and 90-second timer after accessories — stick to it.
Use a timer for every rest period today: 2.5 minutes between main lift sets, 90 seconds between accessory sets — enforce the structure.
tasks.why: Timed rest periods drive training density, which is a key variable for hypertrophy in month 2.

variants: After your session, log every set in Strong or Hevy and write a 2-sentence session note: one thing that improved today, one thing to fix next time.
Post-workout logging: enter all sets and add a 2-line session note to your training app — a win and a priority for next session.
Close out today's log in Strong or Hevy with every set recorded and a quick note — what improved, what's the focus for next time.
tasks.why: Session notes turn training into a learning loop — every workout should inform the next one.

variants: Log your weight and note any joint soreness — if knees, elbows, or shoulders are consistently nagging, that's a signal to deload next week.
Morning weigh-in: log the number, then do a 30-second body scan — are any joints sore in a way that's different from muscle fatigue?
Weigh in and record it, then check in with your joints — persistent joint soreness (not muscle soreness) is a warning sign to address before it becomes injury.
tasks.why: Catching joint issues at 7 weeks prevents the forced deload that happens when they become injuries at week 10.

variants: Pre-log your meals today and add a dedicated recovery meal at night — high protein, complex carbs — to fuel overnight muscle repair.
Build today's food plan in MyFitnessPal and include a solid pre-sleep meal: 30g+ protein, some carbs — casein protein, cottage cheese, or Greek yogurt works.
Set up today's nutrition with a planned evening meal that has 30g+ protein — your muscles repair overnight and they need the amino acids.
tasks.why: Pre-sleep protein reduces muscle protein breakdown during the overnight fast.

variants: Execute today's session — on any lift where you've been stuck for 3+ sessions at the same weight, try adding just 2.5 lbs instead of 5 to break through.
Train today and on any stalled lift, attempt a microload — add 2.5 lbs instead of 5 — to find the progression step that keeps you moving forward.
Hit your workout and use microloading on any lift that hasn't progressed in 3 sessions — 2.5 lb jumps are valid and often unlock a stall.
tasks.why: Microloading is the solution to lifts that stall between standard increment jumps.

variants: Log your weight and look up what a deload week is for your program — if you've trained 7-8 weeks straight, a deload next week may be appropriate.
Morning weigh-in and program check: search how your specific program handles deloads — read it and decide if week 9 should be lighter.
Weigh in, log it, and read your program's deload guidance — 7-8 weeks of progressive overload typically calls for a strategic lighter week.
tasks.why: Planned deloads prevent accumulated fatigue from masking strength gains and causing overuse injuries.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal and specifically confirm you're eating enough carbs on training days — carbs under 150g on a training day is too low.
Set up today's food plan and check your carbohydrate total — a training day should have 150g+ carbs to fuel performance and glycogen replenishment.
Build today's meal plan with adequate carbs for training — log it in MyFitnessPal and verify carbs are above 150g for a full training day.
tasks.why: Carbohydrates are the primary fuel source for high-intensity lifting — inadequate carbs tank training performance.

variants: Execute your full session today including all accessories — log it all, note your PRs, and after training write your best lift performance from Day 10 vs. today.
Train today and after the session, look up your Day 10 log in Strong or Hevy — compare weights for your main lifts then vs. now and write the differences.
Run your complete workout, then pull up your Day 10 session in your training app — compare then vs. now on 3 lifts and write down the progress.
tasks.why: Comparing to Day 10 shows 38 days of accumulated strength gains in concrete numbers.

variants: Week 7 review: how many sessions this week, did every compound lift progress, and are you on pace for your Day 60 targets?
Seven-week training review: count sessions completed, list lifts that progressed, and check whether your current rate of gain will hit your Day 60 strength goals.
Weekly audit: sessions done vs. planned, lift-by-lift progress check, and a quick calculation of whether you'll hit your Day 60 targets at this pace.
tasks.why: Seven-week review with an eye toward Day 60 targets ensures the second half is planned, not improvised.

variants: Take your week 7 progress photos, compare them to Day 1 and Day 30, and write a 3-sentence assessment of how your physique has changed since the midpoint.
Week 7 photo check: 3 angles, saved next to your archive — compare to Day 30 specifically and describe the change in one sentence per angle.
Shoot your 7-week photos from all 3 angles and line them up with your Day 30 photos — you're in the second half now, what has changed?
tasks.why: Comparing week 7 to day 30 shows only the second-half progress, which is motivating and informative.

variants: Nutrition review for week 7: average protein, average calories, number of days you hit your protein target — compare it to your week 4 numbers.
Pull week 7 nutrition data from MyFitnessPal: daily protein average, calorie average, and days hitting target — is it better or worse than week 4?
Week 7 nutrition audit: calculate your 7-day average protein and calories, count your protein hit days, and compare the numbers to your month 1 data.
tasks.why: Tracking nutrition compliance across months reveals whether your habits are improving or drifting.

variants: Log your weight and calculate the average for the past 7 days — compare this week's 7-day average to your week 5 average to see the clean trend.
Morning weigh-in: log it and do a 7-day average comparison between this week and 2 weeks ago — this is the real progress signal.
Step on scale, record it, and pull your 7-day average — compare it to your 7-day average from week 5 to measure the actual weight trend.
tasks.why: Comparing 7-day averages two weeks apart cuts through all daily noise and gives you the real trend.

variants: Look up how to incorporate a deload week into your program and plan it for next week if you've been training 7+ weeks without a break.
Research your program's deload protocol and if week 8 is coming up with no scheduled break, plan a lighter training week now — reduce weights by 40-50%.
Decide on a deload week: if you've completed 7 straight weeks of progressive overload, plan next week as a deload — 40-50% weight, same movements.
tasks.why: A planned deload at week 8 is where you recover accumulated fatigue and come back stronger for the final phase.

variants: Train today per the program and go for a PR on your primary lift — you're 50 days in, the numbers should be significantly above where you started.
Execute today's session and specifically attempt a 50-day PR on your strongest compound lift — what's the most weight you've moved in this program?
Hit your workout and mark the 50-day point with a PR attempt on your main lift — log it and celebrate it regardless of the outcome.
tasks.why: Day 50 is a milestone — setting a PR here documents real, measurable progress.

variants: Log your weight and verify your calorie target is still appropriate — after 50 days your bodyweight may have increased enough to recalculate protein and TDEE.
Morning weigh-in: if your bodyweight has changed by more than 5 lbs from Day 1, recalculate your TDEE and protein target today.
Step on scale, log it, and check: has your weight increased 5+ lbs from Day 1? If so, your protein and calorie targets need to be recalculated.
tasks.why: As bodyweight increases, maintenance calories and protein targets increase with it — recalculate at major milestones.

variants: Recalculate your protein target using your current bodyweight: current weight × 0.8 = new daily gram target — update MyFitnessPal if the number changed.
Update your nutrition targets for the final phase: calculate protein at 0.8g per lb of your current weight and update your MyFitnessPal daily goal.
Current weight × 0.8 = your updated protein target — log it, compare to your current MyFitnessPal setting, and adjust if needed.
tasks.why: Protein needs scale with bodyweight — recalculating ensures you're not undereating protein as you add mass.

variants: Rest day: spend 6 minutes reviewing your training log for the past 3 weeks and count how many individual exercise PRs you've set — write the total.
Active recovery: review Strong or Hevy for the past 3 weeks and log every PR you set — weight PRs and rep PRs both count.
Rest day progress check: open your training app and count every PR from the past 3 weeks — list the lift and the PR number.
