tasks.why: Counting PRs over a 3-week window quantifies progress and motivates continued effort.

variants: If this is your deload week, today's session should be at 50% of your usual working weight — same exercises, same sets and reps, much lighter.
Deload session today: run your normal workout but with 40-50% of your regular weights — focus on perfect technique, not effort.
Deload day: train all your normal movements but cut weight in half — this is active recovery, not an easy workout.
tasks.why: A deload at 50% weight maintains movement patterns while clearing accumulated fatigue from the nervous system.

variants: Use the lighter deload session to fix technique: on every set, focus entirely on form, not effort — note any movement that feels different at a lighter weight.
Deload training tip: at lighter weights today, slow every rep down to 3 seconds on the lowering phase — it's a technique session, not a muscle session.
During your deload session, use the lighter loads to practice perfect form on every rep — write one technique observation per lift after the session.
tasks.why: Deload sessions are the perfect time to ingrain technique improvements that get sloppy under heavy loads.

variants: Post-deload session: plan month 3's training approach — will you continue your current program, switch to a new split, or change rep ranges? Write your plan.
After today's easy session, spend 8 minutes planning the final phase (days 61-90): same program, modified program, or new split — write down your decision.
Use your deload session's extra energy to plan month 3: look at your options (continue current program, switch to higher rep work, change the split) and commit to one.
tasks.why: Planning month 3 during the deload means you walk into day 61 with a clear direction.

variants: Log your weight and note that deload week often shows a slight weight dip — this is normal water and glycogen fluctuation, not muscle loss.
Morning weigh-in during deload: log the number and remember that any weight dip is glycogen, not muscle — it comes back in week 9.
Step on scale, log it, and don't be alarmed by a slight dip this week — deloads temporarily reduce glycogen stores which affects scale weight.
tasks.why: Understanding weight fluctuations during deload prevents misinterpreting data as muscle loss.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal — during deload week you can slightly reduce calories by 100-150 since training volume is lower.
Build today's food plan and reduce calories slightly for the deload week — 100 fewer calories is fine since you're not training as hard.
Set up today's nutrition in MyFitnessPal and reduce daily calories by 100-150 this week — less training volume means slightly less fuel needed.
tasks.why: Minor calorie reduction during a deload week prevents unnecessary fat gain while training volume is down.

variants: Research the program you'll run in month 3 — look at the specific split, rep ranges, and weekly structure and write out week 1's template.
If you're switching programs for month 3, spend 8 minutes reading the full program overview and write your first week's session structure.
Plan month 3's Week 1 template: what sessions, what lifts, what rep ranges — write it out so day 61 has a complete plan ready to execute.
tasks.why: Having month 3 planned before the deload ends means zero friction in the return to full training.

variants: Log your weight and note that coming out of deload, your performance next week will likely exceed anything you've done before.
Morning weigh-in: log it and mentally prepare for week 9 — deloads prime your body to set PRs in the sessions that follow.
Step on scale, record it, and get ready — week 9 after the deload typically produces your best strength performances yet.
tasks.why: Post-deload sessions are often the strongest of any training block — anticipating this fuels the return.

variants: Deload session today: run your full workout at reduced weight, focus on controlled eccentric movements, and note how much easier the movement feels.
Train today at deload weight and use the lightness to feel the full range of motion on every rep — slow and controlled throughout.
Deload workout: move at 50% weight, 3-second eccentrics on every rep, and pay attention to technique details you normally can't notice under load.
tasks.why: The deload session that focuses on technique turns lighter training into a setup for stronger performance next week.

variants: Rest: review your planned month 3 program and finalize the starting weights you'll use for each lift — write them into your training app.
Enter next week's session into Strong or Hevy with the weights you plan to open month 3 with — use your best recent weights as the starting point.
Prepare month 3's first session in your training app: exercises, starting weights, sets and reps — have it ready to go when week 9 starts.
tasks.why: Entering week 9's session now means you walk in knowing exactly what to lift.

variants: Pre-log today's full meals in MyFitnessPal and build back to your normal calorie surplus — the deload is almost done, increase calories to target today.
Resume your full calorie surplus today: build your meal plan in MyFitnessPal back to the 200-300 over maintenance level.
Set up today's nutrition at your full target calories — deload reduced it slightly, but today you return to your normal surplus in MyFitnessPal.
tasks.why: Restoring full calorie intake before the deload ends primes glycogen and is ready for week 9's hard training.

variants: Final deload session: run the workout at 50% weight, and after each exercise write one technique note you noticed that you'll carry into month 3.
Last deload training day: move all your normal lifts at reduced weight and collect one technical cue per lift to apply in the fresh month 3 sessions.
Complete your final deload session today — at 50% weight, focused entirely on form — and write 3 technique takeaways to carry into the next training block.
tasks.why: The final deload session is the last chance to ingrain technique before weights go back up.

variants: Take your 8-week progress photos — 3 angles — and compare them to Day 1, Day 30, and Day 45 for the full timeline of change.
Week 8 photo check: front, side, back — saved and labeled — compare to your Day 1 and Day 30 photos and note the clearest change.
Shoot your 8-week progress photos and line them up with your full archive: Day 1, Week 4, Week 6 — write a sentence about the change you see at each comparison.
tasks.why: Eight-week photos are the most striking comparison because they span nearly the full program.

variants: Week 8 review: list every lift from the deload week, note which ones felt notably lighter, and set the weights for your post-deload sessions.
End of deload week review: look at your logged sessions, confirm the weights, and prepare your mind for week 9's full-effort return.
Eight-week training summary: how many total sessions have you completed across all 8 weeks? Write the number and calculate your consistency rate.
tasks.why: Deload week review captures technique improvements made under lighter loads before returning to heavy work.

variants: Full 8-week nutrition review: average daily protein across the entire 8 weeks — how many of those days did you hit your target?
Calculate your overall protein compliance rate for the first 8 weeks: days you hit your protein target ÷ 56 × 100 = your rate. Write it down.
Eight-week nutrition audit: total days logged in MyFitnessPal, days hitting protein target, and your overall average daily protein — write all three numbers.
tasks.why: An 8-week nutrition compliance rate tells you exactly how much of your progress was supported by diet.

variants: Take your 8-week body measurements — arms, chest, waist — compare to your Day 1, Day 32, and Day 40 measurements and calculate the changes.
Full measurement audit at 8 weeks: arms, chest, and waist — compare to your earliest measurement and write the gains or changes for each site.
Week 8 measurement day: tape measure all 3 sites, record them, and compare to your Day 1 baseline — list the change in inches or centimeters for each.
tasks.why: Eight weeks of measurement data shows body composition changes that the scale alone never captures.

variants: Post-deload first day back: log your weight and get ready — today's session will feel easier than it should, which is the point of deloads.
Morning weigh-in back from deload week: log the number and mentally prepare for your return to full training today.
Step on scale, log it, and get fired up — this is the first full-effort session after deload, and it should feel like a reset.
tasks.why: The post-deload session is typically your strongest — capitalizing on it sets the tone for month 3.

variants: Pre-log today's full meals in MyFitnessPal at your normal surplus target — training resumes at full intensity today.
Build today's complete meal plan in MyFitnessPal: full protein target, full surplus calories — you're back in full training mode.
Set up today's nutrition at your normal targets in MyFitnessPal — deload is over, the full surplus and protein target are back on.
tasks.why: Returning to full nutrition on the first day back from deload maximizes the post-deload performance spike.

variants: Execute your first post-deload session at full intensity — expect to feel strong, and capitalize on it by attempting a PR on your main lift.
Train at 100% today — the deload should make you feel fresher than week 8's last session — attempt a PR on your primary compound lift.
First full-intensity session back: run the workout hard, chase a PR on your top lift, and log everything in Strong or Hevy — this is what deloads are for.
tasks.why: Post-deload sessions are the strongest of any training block — you've earned this performance by resting smart.

variants: Log your weight post-deload — it may be slightly up from water and glycogen returning, which is a good sign that recovery worked.
Morning weigh-in: log it and expect a slight uptick from deload — glycogen restoration adds 1-2 lbs of water weight, which is normal and good.
Step on scale and log it — any post-deload weight spike is glycogen, not fat — it means your muscles are fully fueled for month 3.
tasks.why: Post-deload weight uptick is a sign of successful glycogen restoration, not fat gain.

variants: Pre-log today's meals in MyFitnessPal and ensure carbs are substantial today — post-deload training requires maximum glycogen.
Build today's meal plan with carbs prioritized — 180g+ on a full training day — and log it in MyFitnessPal before eating.
Set up today's nutrition with high carbs for training: 180g+ carbs logged in MyFitnessPal, with the biggest servings around your workout window.
tasks.why: Post-deload training days need maximum carb support to capitalize on the strength boost.

variants: Execute today's session and if you set a PR yesterday, aim to equal or exceed it today on a different lift.
Train today and carry the post-deload momentum — attempt a PR on a second lift, different from yesterday's — log it all.
Hit your second post-deload session and continue the PR chase on a different compound lift — this week should be your strongest of the program so far.
tasks.why: The post-deload window lasts 3-5 sessions — maximize it by attempting PRs on multiple lifts.

variants: Log your weight and review the PRs you set this week post-deload — write the new numbers in your notes as your updated benchmarks.
Morning weigh-in: log it, then open your training log and document every PR you set this week — weight PRs and rep PRs both count.
Step on scale, record it, and list this week's PRs in your notes — every new personal record is the new starting point for the next block.
tasks.why: Documenting post-deload PRs establishes the new baseline for month 3's progression.

variants: Pre-log today's meals and verify your updated protein target from Day 51 is still set in MyFitnessPal — confirm the numbers are current.
Build today's food plan and double-check that your MyFitnessPal protein goal reflects your current bodyweight, not your Day 1 weight.
Set up today's nutrition in MyFitnessPal and confirm your protein target is updated — current weight × 0.8, not Day 1 weight × 0.8.
tasks.why: Nutrition targets must stay current with your body — stale numbers lead to underfeeding muscle growth.

variants: Rest day: take your 9-week progress photos — 3 angles — and note this is the last photo set before the Day 60 milestone check.
Active recovery plus photos: take your pre-Day 60 progress photos (front, side, back), compare to your most recent set, and write one observation.
Rest day photo session: 3 angles, saved and labeled 'Day 59', compared to Day 30 — one more day until the Day 60 milestone check.
tasks.why: Pre-milestone photos let you see progress clearly before the Day 60 formal assessment.

variants: Day 60 milestone: weigh yourself, take your 3 progress photos, and calculate the total weight added since Day 1 — this is your 2-month progress report.
Two-month checkpoint: morning weigh-in, full set of progress photos from all 3 angles, and a written record of your weight change from Day 1.
Day 60 formal assessment: log your weight, take photos from front, side, and back, and calculate the difference from your Day 1 weight — write it down.
tasks.why: Day 60 is the second major milestone — documenting it completely gives you the data to make month 3 exceptional.

variants: Review your Day 40 strength targets: did you hit them? Write your actual weights vs. your targets for bench, squat, deadlift, and overhead press.
Compare today's weights against the Day 60 targets you set on Day 40 — write 'hit', 'missed', or 'exceeded' for each lift and calculate by how much.
Pull up the Day 60 strength goals you set on Day 40 and compare them to today's actual weights — write the target, the actual, and the gap or surplus.
tasks.why: Day 60 target review shows exactly how accurate your goal-setting was and calibrates Day 90 targets.

variants: Write your full 2-month report: strength gains on every main lift (with numbers), body measurements change, weight gained, and the 3 biggest lessons learned — then set your Day 90 targets.
Month 2 complete: write your progress summary (lifts, measurements, weight trend, compliance rate) and define 3 specific targets for the final 30 days.
Day 60 full assessment and Day 90 planning: document everything you've achieved in 60 days and write your final 3 goals — a strength target, a physique goal, and a habit to lock in.
tasks.why: The 60-day report and 90-day target setting transforms the final month from training into a mission.

variants: Month 3 starts today — open your training app and confirm your month 3 program is loaded with the correct starting weights from your Day 60 PRs.
First session of month 3: pull up Strong or Hevy and verify the weights for today's session reflect your post-deload PRs, not older numbers.
Month 3 launch: log your weight, open your workout app, and check that today's weights are loaded at your best recent levels.
tasks.why: Month 3 builds on month 2's peak performance — starting weights must reflect your current strength, not where you were 4 weeks ago.

variants: Execute month 3's first session and apply all the technique cues you've collected over 60 days — this is refined training now, not beginner lifting.
Train today at your full capability, using every technique refinement from your 60 days of form work — execute with precision, not just effort.
Hit your first month 3 session with all the technical knowledge from the past 60 days — form is an asset now, not an afterthought.
tasks.why: Month 3 is execution at a higher level — technique, progressive overload, and nutrition all refined.

variants: After today's session, write your Day 90 strength targets based on your current weights — add a realistic but aggressive number to each main lift.
Post-session: set your Day 90 targets for bench, squat, deadlift, and overhead press — current weight plus 30 days of progression at your recent rate.
Write your 4 Day 90 strength goals right now after training: pick a weight for each main compound lift that would require consistent effort to reach.
tasks.why: Day 90 targets give the final month a direction — every session should feel like it's building toward those numbers.

variants: Log your weight and confirm your calorie surplus is back to your normal target after the deload week adjustment.
Morning weigh-in: log the number and double-check your MyFitnessPal calorie target is at your standard surplus, not the reduced deload level.
Step on scale, record it, and verify your nutrition targets are set correctly in MyFitnessPal — full surplus, not deload reduction.
tasks.why: Confirming full nutrition targets on day 2 of month 3 ensures you don't accidentally undereat coming out of the deload.

variants: Take your monthly measurements for month 3 baseline — arms, chest, waist — and compare them to your Day 60 measurements.
Pull out the tape measure: arms, chest, and waist — log the numbers and compare to your Day 56 measurements to start month 3 with a clear baseline.
Month 3 starting measurements: arms, chest, waist — record them and line them up with your previous measurement check from Day 40.
tasks.why: Month 3 measurement baseline lets you track the final phase's body composition changes precisely.

variants: Execute today's training session — if you switched programs for month 3, follow the new split exactly; if continuing, apply your normal progression rule.
Train today per your month 3 plan and add weight per your progression rule on every eligible lift — month 3 is not a maintenance phase.
Run your full session today, applying your progression rule on every lift where you completed all reps last time — log everything in Strong or Hevy.
tasks.why: Month 3 training carries the same progressive overload discipline as months 1 and 2 — the rule doesn't change, the weights do.

variants: Week 9 review: list every session completed, every lift that progressed, and whether you set any PRs coming out of the deload.
Nine-week review: how many sessions this week, which lifts went up, and are your weights now higher than any point before the deload?
Weekly audit: sessions completed, lift progression, and a comparison of this week's weights to your highest pre-deload numbers.
tasks.why: Week 9 review confirms the deload worked — weights should be at or above pre-deload bests.

variants: Take your week 9 progress photos — 3 angles — and compare them to your Day 60 photos specifically to see what month 3 is starting with.
Week 9 photo check: front, side, back — saved next to your Day 60 set — write one sentence about what you see entering the final phase.
Shoot your 9-week photos from all 3 angles and do a side-by-side with your Day 60 baseline — note any change in muscle fullness or definition.
tasks.why: Comparing week 9 to Day 60 isolates the changes made in just the past 9 days.

variants: Nutrition review for week 9: average protein, days hitting target, and whether your calorie average is back to full surplus after the deload week.
Pull week 9 nutrition data from MyFitnessPal: daily protein average, calorie average, and count of protein-hit days — compare to week 8 numbers.
Week 9 nutrition audit: check every logged day, calculate 7-day average protein and calories, and confirm you're back on full targets.
tasks.why: Week 9 nutrition review confirms your fueling is back to full power after the deload week's reduction.

variants: Log your weight and check if you're on track to hit your Day 90 targets — calculate how much weight you need to add to your main lifts each week.
Morning weigh-in: log it, then check your Day 90 strength targets — at your current weekly progression rate, will you hit them?
Step on scale, record it, then calculate: how many weeks until Day 90? How much do your lifts need to increase per week to hit your targets?
tasks.why: Reverse-engineering Day 90 targets from your current weights and weekly progression keeps training purposeful.

variants: Film a form check video for your most improved lift from the past 60 days — verify the technique has improved as much as the weight has.
Record 3 reps of the lift you're most proud of improving over 60 days — watch it and compare to how you imagine your form looked on Day 1.
Shoot a short form video of your strongest lift right now — watch it critically and note one remaining technique issue to continue working on.
tasks.why: Form check on your strongest lift ensures strength gains weren't built on flawed movement patterns.

variants: Execute today's full session and log it — after the workout, compare today's weights to your Day 14 weights on the same lifts.
Train today and post-session: pull up your Day 14 log in Strong or Hevy and compare those weights to today's — calculate the total added across all lifts.
Hit your workout, then after the session look up Day 14's session weights and write the difference for each lift — 50 days of progressive overload in numbers.
tasks.why: Comparing to Day 14 gives you a 50-day view of strength progress — the numbers at this stage are motivating.

variants: Log your weight and assess sleep quality this week — month 3 training should be your hardest yet, which means recovery must be your best yet.
Morning weigh-in: log the number, then rate your sleep for the past 4 nights — in month 3, sleep is your highest-leverage recovery tool.
Step on scale, record it, and evaluate sleep: how many 7.5+ hour nights this week? In the final phase, sleep is non-negotiable.
tasks.why: Month 3 training is the most demanding — recovery quality in the final phase determines how much progress you lock in.

variants: Pre-log today's meals and include a deliberate pre-sleep protein source — cottage cheese, Greek yogurt, or a casein shake — to support overnight recovery.
Build today's food plan in MyFitnessPal with a planned pre-sleep protein source: 25-30g protein 30 minutes before bed, logged now.
Set up today's nutrition and schedule a nighttime protein meal — 25g+ casein or slow-digesting protein before sleep — to maximize overnight muscle repair.
tasks.why: Pre-sleep protein is especially valuable in month 3 when training stress and recovery demands are highest.

variants: Rest day: 15-minute walk and a full review of this week's training — are you on track for your Day 90 strength targets based on this week's numbers?
Active recovery: 15-minute walk plus 5 minutes checking your Day 90 strength progress — how many pounds away are you from each target?
Rest day check-in: walk 15 minutes, then calculate the gap between today's lifts and your Day 90 targets — how many weeks of normal progression will close it?
tasks.why: Regular target gap analysis keeps the final month driven by numbers, not just effort.

variants: Pre-log today's full meals in MyFitnessPal and verify your protein and calorie totals are both on target for a hard training day.
Build today's complete food plan in MyFitnessPal: protein at or above target, calories in surplus — check both numbers before eating.
Set up today's nutrition with full attention: protein at your updated target, calories in your normal surplus, logged in MyFitnessPal before the first meal.
tasks.why: Month 3 nutrition must match month 3 training demands — no shortcuts on fueling in the final phase.

variants: Execute today's training session and push the pace — in month 3, you should be training at higher intensity than any previous month.
Train today at full intensity and challenge yourself to hit a session PR — more weight, more reps, or denser rest periods than your personal best.
Hit your workout today and make it the hardest session you've had in the program — intensity in month 3 should exceed anything from months 1 or 2.
tasks.why: Month 3 is the final push — training intensity should reflect the accumulated fitness from 9 weeks of progressive overload.

variants: After today's session, take a 5-minute mobility cooldown and write your session debrief: best lift, hardest set, one thing to improve next session.

variants: Execute today's session and specifically aim to beat your Day 21 weights on every lift — you've had 46 days of progressive overload since then.

variants: Execute today's session and film a quick form check on your most recently improved lift — verify the technique is holding under heavier month 3 weights.

tasks.why: Planning your next program before Day 90 ends eliminates the gap between programs that leads to detraining.

variants: Log your weight and take your final pre-Day-90 measurements — arms, chest, waist — to have a comparison point for the Day 90 report.
Morning weigh-in and final measurements: tape measure arms, chest, and waist — log these for the Day 90 report so you have the most accurate final numbers.
Step on scale, log it, then take your Day 88 body measurements — arms, chest, waist — these will feed into your Day 90 final assessment.
tasks.why: Pre-Day-90 measurements captured 2 days early give you final numbers before the celebration disrupts the data.

variants: Pre-log today's complete meals in MyFitnessPal — 2 days left, execute with the same discipline as Day 1.
Build today's food plan: every meal logged, protein at target, calories in surplus — 2 days from the end is not the time to let nutrition slip.
Set up today's nutrition in MyFitnessPal with full attention — 2 more days of perfect fueling closes out the program exactly as it started.
tasks.why: Two days of perfect nutrition before the Day 90 assessment maximizes how you look and feel at the finish line.

variants: Execute today's penultimate training session — make it the best session of the program, leave nothing in reserve.
Train today as if it's your last session of the program — full effort, every set, every rep — because Day 90 is the celebration, today is the performance.
Hit your second-to-last session of the program at maximum effort — full intensity, full focus, every set logged — Day 90 is tomorrow.
tasks.why: The second-to-last session is often the best one — urgency and accumulated fitness combine.

variants: Log your weight one final time before Day 90 — tomorrow is the official weigh-in and photo day, today is preparation.
Morning weigh-in on the day before the finish: log the number and note that tomorrow is the Day 90 final assessment.
Step on scale, record it — tomorrow is Day 90, the final weigh-in, final photos, and final strength test.
tasks.why: Day 89 weigh-in gives you a preview of tomorrow's final number.

variants: Pre-log today's meals — your last full nutrition day before Day 90 — and execute it perfectly.
Build today's complete food plan in MyFitnessPal: last full nutrition day, every gram counted, protein above target.
Set up today's nutrition with everything you have — final full nutrition day, logged in advance, protein and calories both on target.
tasks.why: The last full nutrition day before the Day 90 assessment sets up optimal muscle fullness for the final photos and weigh-in.

variants: Final training session of the program — run it completely, set whatever PRs you have left in you, and log every last set in Strong or Hevy.
Last session: hit everything on the program, attempt a PR on any lift you haven't maxed yet this week, and log the final sets of the program.
Close out your training log with your final session — every exercise, every set, every weight — and attempt at least one PR in this last workout.
tasks.why: The final training session of the 90 days deserves maximum effort — it's the last line of your progress story.

variants: Day 90 final assessment: weigh yourself, take your 3 progress photos, and line them up next to your Day 1 baseline — write the total weight change.
90-day milestone: morning weigh-in, progress photos from all 3 angles, and a full comparison to Day 1 — document everything before you celebrate.
Final day: step on scale, take your Day 90 photos (front, side, back), calculate the total weight change from Day 1, and save everything in one place.
tasks.why: Day 90 documentation is the capstone — the complete record of what 90 days of consistent training and nutrition produces.

variants: Write your 90-day strength report: Day 1 weights vs. Day 90 weights for bench, squat, deadlift, and overhead press — calculate the total pounds added per lift.
Final strength audit: find your first session's weights and today's weights for every compound lift — write the progression and calculate total strength gained.
90-day strength summary: list every main lift with your Day 1 starting weight and your current best — calculate the total weight added across all four lifts.
tasks.why: The strength report is the objective proof of what progressive overload produces over 90 days.

variants: Write your complete 90-day program report: weight gained, muscle measurements change, total sessions completed, protein compliance rate, and 3 things that made the difference — then write your next block's start date.
Final report: body weight change, measurements change, strength gains on every lift, consistency rate, top 3 lessons learned, and the date your next training block begins.
Day 90 full assessment: compile your complete results — weight, measurements, lifts, compliance — into a written summary and commit to a next block start date in writing today.
tasks.why: A complete written report on Day 90 closes the loop on 90 days of effort and sets the foundation for the next block — the work doesn't stop here.
